stem: What is the primary purpose of a 'free cover limit' in a group risk scheme?
options: To cap the maximum benefit payable under the scheme.
To set the level of benefit below which no individual medical underwriting is required.
To determine the minimum number of members required for the scheme.
To establish the maximum commission payable to the intermediary.
explanation: The free cover limit (FCL) is the maximum level of benefit for which an individual member can be covered without the need for individual medical underwriting, based on the overall risk profile of the group.

stem: Which of the following factors is most likely to influence the level of a scheme's free cover limit?
options: The employer's industry sector.
The total sum at risk and the number of scheme members.
The deferred period selected by the employer.
The commission structure agreed with the intermediary.
explanation: The free cover limit is primarily influenced by the total sum at risk under the scheme and the number of members, as these factors determine the spread of risk available to the insurer.

stem: An employer wishes to include only employees who have completed 12 months' service in their group life scheme. This is an example of
options: a benefit basis restriction.
an eligibility condition.
a pre-existing condition exclusion.
a proportionate benefit reduction.
explanation: Restricting membership to employees who have completed a qualifying period of service is a common eligibility condition used in group risk schemes.

stem: Under a group life scheme, the 'actively at work' condition typically requires that the member is
options: working a minimum of 16 hours per week.
capable of performing the full duties of their normal occupation.
present at their normal place of work on the date cover is due to commence.
not receiving any form of medical treatment.
explanation: The actively at work condition typically requires that the member is capable of performing the full and normal duties of their occupation, rather than simply being physically present at work.

stem: If a member is not actively at work on the date a new group income protection scheme commences, what is the usual consequence?
options: The member is permanently excluded from the scheme.
The member's cover will not start until they return to active work.
The member receives cover at a reduced benefit level.
The insurer will automatically underwrite the member individually.
explanation: Members who are not actively at work when a scheme commences will typically not receive cover until they satisfy the actively at work requirement by returning to full duties.

stem: When an individual member's benefit exceeds the free cover limit, the insurer will typically require
options: the employer to pay an additional loading on the entire scheme premium.
the member to complete a personal medical questionnaire.
the member to be excluded from the scheme.
the employer to reduce the member's benefit to the free cover limit.
explanation: Where benefits exceed the free cover limit, individual medical underwriting is required, which typically starts with completion of a personal medical questionnaire by the member.

stem: Following individual medical underwriting, an insurer offers cover to a member subject to a premium loading. This means the insurer will
options: exclude the member from the scheme entirely.
provide full cover but charge an additional premium for that member's excess benefit.
provide cover only up to the free cover limit.
defer the member's entry to the scheme by 12 months.
explanation: A premium loading means the insurer accepts the risk but at an increased premium to reflect the additional risk presented by that individual member's health or circumstances.

stem: Non-disclosure during individual medical underwriting for a group life scheme may result in the insurer
options: voiding the entire group policy.
refusing to pay any benefit for the member in question above the free cover limit.
automatically increasing the premium for all scheme members.
requiring the employer to reimburse all previous claims.
explanation: Non-disclosure during individual underwriting for amounts above the free cover limit would typically result in the insurer only paying up to the free cover limit, rather than voiding the entire group policy.

stem: Which type of group risk benefit is most commonly subject to a deferred period?
options: Group life assurance.
Group critical illness.
Group income protection.
Group dependant's pension.
explanation: Group income protection schemes almost always include a deferred period, which is the waiting period between the onset of incapacity and the commencement of benefit payments.

stem: A group income protection scheme has a deferred period of 26 weeks. This means benefit payments will commence
options: 26 weeks after the member first consults their GP.
26 weeks after the member first becomes incapacitated.
26 weeks after the insurer receives the claim notification.
26 weeks after the member's sick pay from the employer ceases.
explanation: The deferred period runs from the date the member first becomes incapacitated, not from any other trigger point such as notification or the end of sick pay.

stem: A group critical illness scheme that includes a 'survival period' requires the claimant to
options: survive for a specified period after diagnosis before the benefit becomes payable.
have been a scheme member for a minimum period before a claim can be made.
survive to a specified age before the lump sum is paid.
provide evidence of ongoing treatment for the diagnosed condition.
explanation: The survival period requires the claimant to survive for a specified number of days (commonly 14 or 28 days) after diagnosis of the critical illness before the benefit becomes payable.

stem: Under a flexible benefits arrangement, an employee can typically
options: choose their own insurer for group risk benefits.
select the level and type of benefits from a menu within a defined budget.
negotiate the premium rate directly with the insurer.
opt out of all employer-provided benefits without restriction.
explanation: Flexible benefits arrangements allow employees to choose from a menu of benefits, adjusting levels and types of cover within a budget typically set by the employer.

stem: What is the main risk to an insurer when offering group risk benefits through a flexible benefits arrangement?
options: Higher administration costs.
Anti-selection by members.
Reduced premium income.
Increased reinsurance costs.
explanation: Anti-selection (adverse selection) is the primary concern, as members in poorer health may choose higher levels of cover while healthier members may opt for lower levels or opt out entirely.

stem: A group life scheme that is established on a 'non-contributory' basis means that
options: the insurer waives the first year's premium.
the employer pays the full cost of the scheme.
the members pay the full cost of the scheme.
no individual underwriting is required.
explanation: A non-contributory scheme is one where the employer pays the full cost of the premiums and members do not contribute towards the cost of cover.

stem: A group risk scheme specifies a maximum entry age of 60. What does this mean for a new employee aged 62?
options: They can join but will pay a higher premium.
They cannot join the scheme.
They can join but only at a reduced benefit level.
They can join subject to satisfactory medical evidence.
explanation: If the scheme specifies a maximum entry age, employees who exceed that age at the point of joining employment are not eligible to join the scheme.

stem: Under a group income protection scheme, the 'proportionate benefit' approach is used to ensure that
options: benefit payments do not exceed a proportion of the member's pre-incapacity earnings when combined with other income.
benefits are split equally between all claimants.
the employer contributes a proportionate share of the premium.
members receive benefits proportionate to their length of service.
explanation: The proportionate benefit approach ensures that the total income a claimant receives from all sources (including state benefits and any other income) does not exceed a set proportion of their pre-incapacity earnings.

stem: In the context of group risk underwriting, what is meant by 'medical evidence at the scheme level'?
options: Every member must complete a medical questionnaire.
The insurer assesses the overall risk profile of the group rather than individual members.
The employer must provide a full occupational health report.
A medical officer must inspect the employer's premises.
explanation: Scheme-level underwriting involves the insurer assessing the overall risk characteristics of the group (such as industry, size, and demographics) rather than requiring individual medical evidence from each member.

stem: In a flexible benefits scheme, the employer typically sets a 'core' level of group life cover. What is the purpose of this core level?
options: To ensure the insurer receives a minimum level of premium.
To provide a minimum level of death benefit for all eligible employees regardless of their benefit choices.
To satisfy the requirements of the Financial Conduct Authority.
To reduce the administrative burden on the scheme trustees.
explanation: The core level ensures all eligible employees have a minimum level of death benefit protection, even if they choose to allocate their flexible benefits budget elsewhere.

stem: An employer operates a group income protection scheme with cover ceasing at age 65. An employee aged 63 becomes incapacitated. What is the maximum period for which benefit could be payable?
options: Two years.
Five years.
Until State Pension age.
There is no maximum period.
explanation: If cover ceases at age 65 and the member becomes incapacitated at age 63, the maximum payment period would be two years (i.e. until the member reaches age 65).

stem: Which of the following is a common exclusion found in group life assurance schemes?
options: Death from natural causes within the first year.
Death resulting from active service in HM Armed Forces.
Death occurring outside the United Kingdom.
Death from a pre-existing heart condition.
explanation: Death resulting from active service in HM Armed Forces is a common exclusion in group life schemes, as this risk is typically covered by the armed forces' own death-in-service arrangements.
